name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
signs:
Chest pain (crushing, pressure-like)
Radiation to left arm, jaw, back
Diaphoresis
Nausea/vomiting
Dyspnea
Fatigue
Syncope
treatment:
1. Activate cath lab (STEMI) - Door-to-balloon <90 min
2. Aspirin 325mg PO STAT
3. P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor 180mg or clopidogrel 600mg)
4. Anticoagulation: heparin or bivalirudin
5. Oxygen if SpO2 <90%
6. Nitrates (unless hypotensive or RV infarct)
7. Beta-blocker if hemodynamically stable
meds:
Aspirin 325mg PO x1 then 81mg daily
Ticagrelor 180mg load then 90mg BID
Metoprolol 25-50mg PO BID
Lisinopril 2.5-10mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 80mg PO daily
Heparin 60u/kg IV bolus (max 4000u)
ddx: NSTEMI, Unstable Angina, Aortic Dissection, PE, Pericarditis, GERD, Musculoskeletal pain
labs:
Troponin I/T (serial q3-6h)
12-lead ECG immediately
CBC, BMP, coags
CXR
Echo
Lipid panel
notes:
STEMI: ST elevation >=1mm in >=2 contiguous leads or new LBBB
Right-sided leads (V3R/V4R) for RV infarct - AVOID nitrates
Inferior MI: check for AV block
Females/diabetics: atypical presentation common

name: Congestive Heart Failure
signs:
Dyspnea on exertion
Orthopnea
Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea
Peripheral edema
JVD
S3 gallop
Pulmonary crackles
Weight gain
Fatigue
treatment:
1. Oxygen if hypoxic
2. IV furosemide for acute decompensation
3. Vasodilators (nitroglycerin) if hypertensive
4. ACEi/ARB or ARNI for HFrEF
5. Beta-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol)
6. Aldosterone antagonist if EF<=35%
7. SGLT2 inhibitor
8. Sodium/fluid restriction
meds:
Furosemide 20-80mg IV/PO
Lisinopril 2.5-40mg daily
Sacubitril/valsartan 24/26mg to 97/103mg BID
Carvedilol 3.125-25mg BID
Spironolactone 12.5-50mg daily
Dapagliflozin 10mg daily
ddx: Pneumonia, COPD exacerbation, PE, Nephrotic syndrome, Cirrhosis, Pericardial effusion
labs:
BNP or NT-proBNP
CXR (cardiomegaly, pulmonary edema)
Echo (EF, wall motion)
BMP (renal function, electrolytes)
CBC
TSH
Iron studies
notes:
HFrEF: EF<=40%, HFmrEF: 41-49%, HFpEF: >=50%
GDAT: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i for HFrEF
Daily weights - report 2lb/day or 5lb/week gain
Avoid NSAIDs, CCBs (except amlodipine) in HFrEF

name: Atrial Fibrillation
signs:
Palpitations
Irregularly irregular pulse
Dyspnea
Fatigue
Dizziness/lightheadedness
Chest discomfort
Exercise intolerance
Syncope (rare)
treatment:
1. Rate control: target HR <110 (or <80 if symptomatic)
2. Rhythm control if symptomatic or new-onset
3. Anticoagulation based on CHA2DS2-VASc score
4. Cardioversion if hemodynamically unstable
5. Catheter ablation for refractory cases
meds:
Metoprolol 25-100mg BID (rate control)
Diltiazem 30-90mg TID or 120-360mg ER daily
Amiodarone 200mg daily (rhythm)
Flecainide 50-150mg BID (rhythm, no structural heart dz)
Apixaban 5mg BID
Rivaroxaban 20mg daily with food
ddx: Atrial flutter, SVT, MAT, Sinus tachycardia with PACs, Hyperthyroidism
labs:
ECG (absent P waves, irregular RR)
TSH
BMP
CBC
Echo
LFTs if starting amiodarone
PT/INR if on warfarin
notes:
CHA2DS2-VASc: CHF, HTN, Age>=75(2), DM, Stroke/TIA(2), Vasc dz, Age 65-74, Sex(female)
Score >=2 men or >=3 women: anticoagulate
48h rule: if AF >48h, anticoagulate 3wk before cardioversion or TEE to r/o thrombus
HAS-BLED score for bleeding risk

name: Aortic Dissection
signs:
Sudden severe tearing/ripping chest pain
Radiation to back (interscapular)
Pulse deficit between arms
BP differential >20mmHg between arms
Aortic regurgitation murmur
Syncope
Stroke symptoms if arch involved
treatment:
1. Type A (ascending): emergent surgical repair
2. Type B (descending): medical management unless complicated
3. IV esmolol or labetalol - target HR <60, SBP 100-120
4. IV nitroprusside after beta-blockade if needed
5. Pain control with IV morphine
6. Avoid anticoagulation until dissection ruled out
meds:
Esmolol 500mcg/kg bolus then 50-200mcg/kg/min
Labetalol 20mg IV q10min (max 300mg)
Nitroprusside 0.25-10mcg/kg/min (after BB)
Morphine 2-4mg IV q5-15min PRN
ddx: MI, PE, Tension pneumothorax, Pericardial tamponade, Esophageal rupture, Mediastinitis
labs:
CT angiography (gold standard)
CXR (widened mediastinum)
TEE if unstable
D-dimer (sensitive but nonspecific)
BMP, CBC, type and screen
Troponin (may elevate if coronary involvement)
notes:
Stanford A: involves ascending aorta - SURGICAL EMERGENCY
Stanford B: descending only - usually medical
Risk factors: HTN, Marfan, bicuspid aortic valve, cocaine
Do NOT delay imaging for labs

name: Hypertensive Emergency
signs:
SBP >180 and/or DBP >120 with end-organ damage
Headache
Visual changes
Chest pain
Dyspnea
Altered mental status
Nausea/vomiting
Focal neurologic deficits
Papilledema
treatment:
1. IV antihypertensives (NOT oral for true emergency)
2. Reduce MAP by 10-20% in first hour
3. Then to 160/100 over next 2-6h
4. Identify and treat underlying cause
5. Avoid rapid or excessive BP reduction
6. Specific agents based on end-organ involvement
meds:
Nicardipine 5-15mg/h IV (most versatile)
Labetalol 20mg IV bolus then 1-2mg/min
Nitroprusside 0.25-10mcg/kg/min (if no ICP concerns)
Clevidipine 1-2mg/h IV
Enalaprilat 1.25mg IV q6h
Fenoldopam 0.1-1.6mcg/kg/min (renal protective)
ddx: Hypertensive urgency (no end-organ damage), Stroke, Pheochromocytoma, Pre-eclampsia, Aortic dissection, Sympathomimetic toxicity
labs:
BMP (creatinine, electrolytes)
Urinalysis (proteinuria, hematuria)
CBC with smear (MAHA)
LDH, haptoglobin
Troponin
CT head if neuro symptoms
CXR
Fundoscopy
notes:
Emergency = end-organ damage; Urgency = severely elevated BP without damage
End organs: brain, heart, kidneys, eyes, aorta
Exception: ischemic stroke - permissive HTN unless >220/120 or getting tPA
Aortic dissection: target SBP 100-120 with beta-blocker first

name: Pericarditis
signs:
Sharp pleuritic chest pain
Improved by sitting up and leaning forward
Worsened by lying supine or deep breathing
Pericardial friction rub
Low-grade fever
Dyspnea
Diffuse ST elevation
treatment:
1. NSAIDs (ibuprofen or aspirin) as first-line
2. Colchicine for 3 months to prevent recurrence
3. Restrict exercise until symptoms resolve and markers normalize
4. Corticosteroids only if NSAID-refractory and not infectious
5. Pericardiocentesis if tamponade
meds:
Ibuprofen 600mg TID x1-2 weeks
Aspirin 750-1000mg TID x1-2 weeks
Colchicine 0.5mg BID (0.5mg daily if <70kg)
Preднизолone 0.25-0.5mg/kg if refractory
PPI for GI protection with NSAIDs
ddx: MI, PE, Aortic dissection, Myocarditis, Pleuritis, Costochondritis, GERD
labs:
ECG (diffuse ST elevation, PR depression)
Troponin (may elevate = myopericarditis)
CRP/ESR
CBC
BMP
Echo (pericardial effusion?)
CXR
Blood cultures if febrile
notes:
Diagnosis: 2 of 4 - chest pain, friction rub, ECG changes, new/worsening pericardial effusion
Viral most common cause (often idiopathic)
Avoid anticoagulation (risk of hemorrhagic pericarditis)
Recurrence rate 30% - colchicine reduces this

name: Pulmonary Embolism
signs:
Sudden dyspnea
Pleuritic chest pain
Tachycardia
Tachypnea
Hemoptysis
Leg swelling (DVT)
Hypotension (massive PE)
Syncope
Hypoxia
treatment:
1. Anticoagulation immediately if high clinical suspicion
2. Submassive: anticoagulation +/- thrombolytics
3. Massive (hemodynamic instability): systemic thrombolysis
4. Surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy if thrombolysis contraindicated
5. IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated
meds:
Heparin 80u/kg bolus then 18u/kg/h
Enoxaparin 1mg/kg SC BID
Apixaban 10mg BID x7d then 5mg BID
Rivaroxaban 15mg BID x21d then 20mg daily
Alteplase 100mg IV over 2h (massive PE)
ddx: MI, Pneumonia, Pneumothorax, Aortic dissection, Pericarditis, Anxiety/panic, Pleuritis
labs:
CT pulmonary angiography (gold standard)
D-dimer (to rule out if low pretest probability)
ECG (sinus tach, S1Q3T3, RBBB, RV strain)
Troponin, BNP (risk stratification)
ABG
Lower extremity duplex US
Echo (RV dilation)
notes:
Wells criteria for pretest probability
Negative D-dimer with low Wells rules out PE
Massive = hypotension; Submassive = RV dysfunction without hypotension
Anticoagulate 3mo minimum; longer if unprovoked or recurrent
Heparin does NOT dissolve clot - prevents extension

name: Deep Vein Thrombosis
signs:
Unilateral leg swelling
Pain/tenderness along deep veins
Warmth
Erythema
Homan sign (unreliable)
Pitting edema
Distension of superficial veins
Low-grade fever
treatment:
1. Anticoagulation for minimum 3 months
2. DOAC preferred over warfarin for most patients
3. Compression stockings for symptom relief
4. Catheter-directed thrombolysis for massive iliofemoral DVT
5. IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated
6. Early ambulation
meds:
Apixaban 10mg BID x7d then 5mg BID
Rivaroxaban 15mg BID x21d then 20mg daily
Enoxaparin 1mg/kg SC BID (bridge to warfarin)
Warfarin target INR 2-3
Heparin 80u/kg bolus then 18u/kg/h
ddx: Cellulitis, Baker cyst rupture, Superficial thrombophlebitis, Lymphedema, Muscle strain, Chronic venous insufficiency, Compartment syndrome
labs:
Compression ultrasonography (gold standard)
D-dimer (rule out if low pretest)
CBC, BMP, PT/INR
Consider thrombophilia workup if unprovoked/recurrent
CT venography if US inconclusive
notes:
Wells score for pretest probability
Proximal DVT (above knee) has higher PE risk than distal
Provoked (surgery, immobilization, cancer) vs unprovoked
Cancer screening recommended for unprovoked DVT in age >40
May-Thurner syndrome: left iliac vein compression

name: Infective Endocarditis
signs:
Fever (most common)
New or changing heart murmur
Osler nodes (painful, fingers/toes)
Janeway lesions (painless, palms/soles)
Splinter hemorrhages
Roth spots (retinal)
Splenomegaly
Petechiae
Embolic events (stroke, septic emboli)
treatment:
1. Empiric antibiotics after 3 sets blood cultures
2. Native valve: vancomycin + ceftriaxone empirically
3. Prosthetic valve: vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin
4. Surgical intervention for heart failure, uncontrolled infection, large vegetations, embolic events
5. 4-6 weeks IV antibiotics typically
meds:
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h
Ceftriaxone 2g IV q24h
Nafcillin 2g IV q4h (MSSA)
Gentamicin 1mg/kg IV q8h
Rifampin 300mg PO q8h (prosthetic valve)
ddx: Rheumatic fever, SLE (Libman-Sacks), Atrial myxoma, Marantic endocarditis, Bacteremia without endocarditis
labs:
Blood cultures x3 (different sites, different times)
TTE then TEE if TTE negative but high suspicion
CBC (anemia, leukocytosis)
ESR/CRP elevated
Urinalysis (hematuria)
Rheumatoid factor
Complement levels
notes:
Modified Duke criteria: 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor
Major: positive blood cultures (typical organisms), endocardial involvement on echo
Minor: predisposition, fever, vascular/immunologic phenomena, suggestive micro
IVDU: right-sided (tricuspid) most common
S. aureus most common overall; Strep viridans in native valve

name: Community-Acquired Pneumonia
signs:
Fever/chills
Productive cough
Dyspnea
Pleuritic chest pain
Tachycardia
Tachypnea
Decreased breath sounds
Dullness to percussion
Egophony
Tactile fremitus increased
treatment:
1. Risk stratify with CURB-65 or PSI/PORT
2. Outpatient: amoxicillin or doxycycline; add macrolide if comorbidities
3. Inpatient non-ICU: beta-lactam + macrolide OR respiratory fluoroquinolone
4. ICU: beta-lactam + macrolide or fluoroquinolone
5. Add MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage if risk factors
6. O2 supplementation if SpO2 <94%
meds:
Amoxicillin 1g PO TID
Azithromycin 500mg then 250mg daily x4d
Ceftriaxone 1-2g IV daily
Levofloxacin 750mg PO/IV daily
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h (if MRSA)
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h (if Pseudomonas)
ddx: HCAP, Aspiration pneumonia, PE, CHF exacerbation, Lung abscess, TB, Lung cancer
labs:
CXR
CBC with differential
BMP
Blood cultures x2 before abx
Sputum culture/Gram stain
Urinary Legionella/Pneumococcal antigen
ProCalcitonin
ABG if severe
notes:
CURB-65: Confusion, Urea >7, RR >=30, BP <90/60, Age >=65
0-1: outpatient; 2: consider admission; >=3: ICU consideration
Atypical organisms: Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila
Follow-up CXR in 6-8 weeks for smokers >50yo

name: COPD Exacerbation
signs:
Increased dyspnea
Increased sputum volume/purulence
Wheezing
Accessory muscle use
Tachypnea
Cyanosis
Decreased breath sounds
Barrel chest
Pursed lip breathing
Peripheral edema (cor pulmonale)
treatment:
1. Bronchodilators (SABA +/- SAMA)
2. Systemic corticosteroids
3. Antibiotics if increased sputum purulence
4. Oxygen target SpO2 88-92%
5. NIV (BiPAP) if respiratory acidosis
6. Intubation if failing NIV
meds:
Albuterol 2.5mg nebulized q20min x3 then q4h
Ipratropium 0.5mg nebulized q4-6h
Prednisone 40mg PO daily x5 days
Azithromycin 500mg then 250mg x4d
Doxycycline 100mg BID x5-7d
ddx: Asthma exacerbation, Pneumonia, PE, CHF, Pneumothorax, Lung cancer
labs:
ABG (hypercapnia, respiratory acidosis)
CXR (hyperinflation, r/o pneumonia/PTX)
CBC
BMP
Sputum culture if purulent
ProCalcitonin
BNP if CHF suspected
notes:
GOLD criteria for severity
ABCs of COPD exacerbation: Antibiotics, Bronchodilators, Corticosteroids
Avoid high-flow O2 - may worsen hypercapnia
BiPAP settings: start IPAP 10, EPAP 5
Escalate IPAP for ventilation (CO2), EPAP for oxygenation

name: Asthma Exacerbation
signs:
Wheezing
Dyspnea
Chest tightness
Cough (often worse at night)
Tachypnea
Accessory muscle use
Difficulty speaking in full sentences
Pulsus paradoxus >10mmHg
Decreased air movement (severe)
treatment:
1. Continuous albuterol nebulization
2. Ipratropium in first hour for severe
3. Systemic corticosteroids early
4. Magnesium sulfate IV for severe/life-threatening
5. Oxygen to target SpO2 93-95%
6. Intubation for respiratory failure (use ketamine for induction)
meds:
Albuterol 2.5-5mg nebulized q20min x3 or continuous 10-15mg/h
Ipratropium 0.5mg nebulized q20min x3
Methylprednisolone 125mg IV or Prednisone 40-60mg PO
Magnesium sulfate 2g IV over 20min
Epinephrine 0.3mg IM (anaphylaxis/severe)
ddx: COPD exacerbation, Vocal cord dysfunction, Foreign body, PE, Anaphylaxis, CHF (cardiac asthma)
labs:
Peak flow (PEF <25% predicted = severe)
SpO2 continuous
ABG if severe
CXR (r/o pneumothorax, pneumonia)
BMP
Consider VBG
notes:
Mild: speaks sentences, PEF >50%
Moderate: speaks phrases, PEF 33-50%
Severe: speaks words, PEF <33%
Life-threatening: silent chest, cyanosis, bradycardia, confusion
Silent chest = critical - means almost no air movement
Avoid sedation unless intubating

name: Pneumothorax
signs:
Sudden onset pleuritic chest pain
Dyspnea
Decreased breath sounds on affected side
Hyperresonance to percussion
Tracheal deviation (tension)
Tachycardia
Hypotension (tension)
Subcutaneous emphysema
treatment:
1. Small primary spontaneous (<2cm): observe, supplemental O2
2. Large or symptomatic: needle aspiration or chest tube
3. Tension pneumothorax: immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL) then chest tube
4. Chest tube: 5th ICS anterior axillary line
5. Pleurodesis or surgery for recurrence
meds:
Supplemental O2 (accelerates reabsorption)
Analgesia: ketorolac 15-30mg IV, morphine 2-4mg IV
Local anesthetic for chest tube: lidocaine 1% 10-20mL
ddx: PE, MI, Pericarditis, Rib fracture, Pleuritis, Aortic dissection, Esophageal rupture
labs:
CXR (visceral pleural line, absent lung markings)
CT chest (if CXR equivocal)
ABG if respiratory distress
Point-of-care ultrasound (absent lung sliding)
notes:
Tension PTX is a CLINICAL diagnosis - do NOT delay for imaging
Primary: no underlying lung disease, tall thin males
Secondary: underlying COPD, CF, etc - higher risk
Iatrogenic: post-central line, post-biopsy, post-mechanical ventilation
Reabsorption rate: ~1.25% per day on room air, faster with O2

name: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome
signs:
Acute onset dyspnea
Hypoxemia refractory to O2
Bilateral pulmonary infiltrates
Tachypnea
Tachycardia
Accessory muscle use
Cyanosis
Decreased lung compliance
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause
2. Lung-protective ventilation: Vt 6mL/kg IBW
3. Plateau pressure <30 cmH2O
4. PEEP titration (ARDSNet table)
5. Prone positioning if P/F <150
6. Conservative fluid strategy
7. Neuromuscular blockade in severe (first 48h)
meds:
Cisatracurium 15mg bolus then 37.5mg/h (paralysis)
Fentanyl 25-100mcg/h + Propofol 5-50mcg/kg/min (sedation)
Furosemide for conservative fluid management
Treat underlying cause (antibiotics for sepsis, etc)
ddx: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema, Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, Acute eosinophilic pneumonia, Pulmonary vasculitis
labs:
ABG (P/F ratio)
CXR/CT (bilateral opacities)
Echo (r/o cardiogenic edema)
BNP
CBC, BMP, lactate
Blood cultures, sputum cultures
notes:
Berlin criteria: acute onset, bilateral opacities, not fully explained by cardiac failure
Mild: P/F 200-300, Moderate: 100-200, Severe: <100
P/F = PaO2/FiO2
Mortality 30-40% overall
Prone positioning 16h/day reduces mortality in severe

name: Ischemic Stroke
signs:
Sudden focal neurologic deficit
Facial droop
Arm/leg weakness (contralateral)
Speech difficulty (aphasia/dysarthria)
Vision loss
Ataxia
Vertigo
Severe headache (less common than hemorrhagic)
treatment:
1. CT head STAT to rule out hemorrhage
2. IV alteplase if within 4.5h of symptom onset
3. Mechanical thrombectomy if LVO within 24h (with imaging selection)
4. Aspirin 325mg within 24-48h (not within 24h of tPA)
5. Permissive HTN (allow up to 220/120 unless tPA given)
6. Admit to stroke unit
7. DVT prophylaxis
meds:
Alteplase 0.9mg/kg IV (max 90mg): 10% bolus, 90% over 60min
Aspirin 325mg PO/PR
Labetalol 10-20mg IV (if BP >185/110 for tPA)
Nicardipine 5mg/h IV titrate
Heparin for DVT prophylaxis after 24h
ddx: Hemorrhagic stroke, TIA, Todd paralysis (postictal), Hypoglycemia, Migraine with aura, Brain tumor, Conversion disorder
labs:
CT head without contrast STAT
CT angiography (head and neck)
CT perfusion (for extended window)
Glucose STAT
CBC, BMP, PT/INR
Troponin
ECG
MRI brain (DWI) when able
notes:
Time is brain: 1.9M neurons lost per minute
tPA window: 4.5h; Exclusions: recent surgery, bleeding, INR >1.7, platelets <100k
NIHSS for severity scoring
Large vessel occlusion: consider thrombectomy up to 24h with favorable imaging
Swallow screen before any PO

name: Hemorrhagic Stroke
signs:
Sudden severe headache (worst of life)
Focal neurologic deficits
Altered mental status
Nausea/vomiting
Seizures
Nuchal rigidity (if SAH)
HTN
Pupil abnormalities
Decorticate/decerebrate posturing (severe)
treatment:
1. CT head STAT
2. Reverse anticoagulation immediately
3. BP management: target SBP <140 for ICH
4. Seizure prophylaxis controversial
5. ICP management: HOB 30 degrees, mannitol or hypertonic saline
6. Neurosurgical consultation
7. EVD if hydrocephalus
8. SAH: secure aneurysm (coiling or clipping)
meds:
Nicardipine 5-15mg/h IV (BP control)
Labetalol 10-20mg IV bolus
4-factor PCC for warfarin reversal
Idarucizumab for dabigatran reversal
Andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitor reversal
Mannitol 20% 1-1.5g/kg IV
Hypertonic saline 23.4% 30mL via central line
Nimodipine 60mg PO q4h (SAH vasospasm prevention)
ddx: Ischemic stroke, Subdural/epidural hematoma, Brain tumor with hemorrhage, Hypertensive encephalopathy
labs:
CT head without contrast
CTA (aneurysm, AVM)
CBC, BMP, coags (PT/INR, PTT)
Type and screen
Lumbar puncture if SAH suspected but CT negative
MRI/MRA when stable
notes:
ICH: most common cause is HTN (basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, cerebellum)
SAH: thunderclap headache, Hunt-Hess and Fisher grading
SAH complications: rebleeding, vasospasm (days 4-14), hydrocephalus
GCS and ICH score for prognosis
Avoid hyperthermia, hyperglycemia, hyponatremia

name: Bacterial Meningitis
signs:
Fever
Headache
Nuchal rigidity (stiff neck)
Altered mental status
Photophobia
Kernig sign (pain with knee extension when hip flexed)
Brudzinski sign (neck flexion causes hip/knee flexion)
Petechial rash (Neisseria)
Seizures
Nausea/vomiting
treatment:
1. Empiric antibiotics IMMEDIATELY - do not delay for LP
2. Dexamethasone before or with first antibiotic dose
3. Blood cultures before antibiotics if possible
4. LP when safe
5. Contact precautions; droplet for N. meningitidis
6. Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts
meds:
Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h
Ampicillin 2g IV q4h (if >50yo or immunocompromised, for Listeria)
Dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg IV q6h x4 days (give before abx)
Rifampin 600mg PO BID x2d (prophylaxis for contacts)
ddx: Viral meningitis, Encephalitis, SAH, Brain abscess, Lyme disease, TB meningitis, Fungal meningitis
labs:
Blood cultures x2
LP: opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture
CSF: WBC >1000, neutrophil predominance, protein >250, glucose <40
CBC, BMP, coags
CT head before LP if: immunocompromised, history of CNS disease, new seizure, papilledema, focal deficits, altered consciousness
notes:
Do NOT delay antibiotics for CT or LP
Common organisms by age:
Neonates: GBS, E. coli, Listeria
Children: N. meningitidis, S. pneumoniae
Adults: S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis
Elderly/immunocompromised: add Listeria coverage (ampicillin)
Vaccination: MenACWY, MenB, PCV13/23

name: Status Epilepticus
signs:
Continuous seizure activity >5 minutes
Recurrent seizures without return to baseline
Tonic-clonic movements
Altered consciousness
Tongue biting
Incontinence
Cyanosis
Tachycardia
HTN
Hyperthermia
treatment:
1. ABCs, protect airway
2. First-line: IV benzodiazepine
3. Second-line: IV fosphenytoin, valproate, or levetiracetam
4. Refractory: continuous midazolam, propofol, or pentobarbital infusion
5. Check glucose, give thiamine before dextrose if indicated
6. Treat underlying cause
7. Continuous EEG monitoring
meds:
Lorazepam 4mg IV (may repeat x1) or Diazepam 10mg IV
Midazolam 10mg IM if no IV access
Fosphenytoin 20mg PE/kg IV (max 150mg PE/min)
Levetiracetam 60mg/kg IV (max 4500mg)
Valproate 40mg/kg IV (max 3000mg)
Midazolam infusion 0.2mg/kg bolus then 0.05-2mg/kg/h
Propofol 2mg/kg bolus then 20-200mcg/kg/min
ddx: Syncope with myoclonus, Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures (PNES), Rigors, Movement disorders, Decerebrate posturing
labs:
Glucose STAT
BMP (Na, Ca, Mg)
CBC
Antiepileptic drug levels
Urine drug screen
ABG/VBG (acidosis)
CT head
LP if infection suspected
Continuous EEG
notes:
5 minutes = status epilepticus (previously 30min)
Time is brain - prolonged seizures cause neuronal injury
Nonconvulsive status: altered consciousness without obvious motor activity - need EEG
Common causes: AED non-compliance, alcohol withdrawal, metabolic, stroke, infection
Mortality 20% in adults

name: Migraine
signs:
Unilateral throbbing headache
Photophobia
Phonophobia
Nausea/vomiting
Aura (visual: scintillating scotoma, zigzag lines)
Duration 4-72 hours
Worsened by activity
Osmophobia
treatment:
1. Acute: triptans first-line for moderate-severe
2. NSAIDs for mild-moderate
3. Antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine)
4. Preventive if >=4 attacks/month or significant disability
5. Avoid medication overuse (limit acute meds to <10-15 days/month)
6. Identify and avoid triggers
meds:
Sumatriptan 50-100mg PO or 6mg SC
Rizatriptan 10mg PO
Ibuprofen 400-800mg PO
Metoclopramide 10mg IV
Ketorolac 30mg IV
Prevention: Propranolol 40-160mg daily, Topiramate 50-100mg daily, Amitriptyline 25-75mg QHS
CGRP mAbs: Erenumab 70-140mg SC monthly
ddx: Tension headache, Cluster headache, SAH, Meningitis, Brain tumor, Temporal arteritis, Sinusitis
labs:
Clinical diagnosis
Neuroimaging if: thunderclap, worst headache, new neuro deficit, fever, papilledema, immunocompromised, age >50 new onset
MRI brain with/without contrast if atypical features
notes:
Aura: typically visual, lasting 5-60min before headache
Migraine without aura is most common type
Medication overuse headache: suspect if headache >15 days/month with frequent acute med use
Triptans contraindicated in hemiplegic migraine, basilar migraine, CAD, uncontrolled HTN
Status migrainosus: migraine lasting >72h

name: Acute Appendicitis
signs:
Periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ
McBurney point tenderness
Rebound tenderness
Rovsing sign
Psoas sign
Obturator sign
Anorexia
Nausea/vomiting
Low-grade fever
Guarding
treatment:
1. Appendectomy (laparoscopic preferred)
2. IV antibiotics preoperatively
3. NPO
4. IV fluids
5. Pain management
6. Antibiotics alone may be considered for uncomplicated cases
7. Percutaneous drainage if phlegmon/abscess, interval appendectomy later
meds:
Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h
OR Ceftriaxone 2g IV + Metronidazole 500mg IV
Ketorolac 15-30mg IV
Morphine 2-4mg IV PRN
Ondansetron 4mg IV PRN nausea
ddx: Mesenteric lymphadenitis, Meckel diverticulitis, Ovarian torsion, Ruptured ovarian cyst, Ectopic pregnancy, Crohn disease, Right-sided diverticulitis, Kidney stone
labs:
CBC (leukocytosis with left shift)
CRP
BMP
Urinalysis (r/o UTI/stone)
Pregnancy test in females
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (gold standard)
US (first-line in children and pregnant)
notes:
Classic progression: vague periumbilical pain -> RLQ over 12-24h
Alvarado score: Migration, Anorexia, Nausea, RLQ tenderness, Rebound, Temperature, Leukocytosis, Shift left
Perforation risk increases after 36-48h
Pregnancy: appendix displaced superiorly
Atypical location with retrocecal appendix

name: Acute Pancreatitis
signs:
Epigastric pain radiating to back
Nausea/vomiting
Abdominal tenderness/guarding
Distension
Fever
Tachycardia
Hypotension (severe)
Cullen sign (periumbilical ecchymosis)
Grey Turner sign (flank ecchymosis)
treatment:
1. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (goal-directed, LR preferred)
2. Pain management
3. NPO initially then early oral feeding as tolerated
4. Treat underlying cause (ERCP for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis)
5. No prophylactic antibiotics for sterile necrosis
6. ICU for organ failure
7. Cholecystectomy during same admission for gallstone pancreatitis
meds:
Lactated Ringer 250-500mL/h initially, goal-directed
Hydromorphone 0.5-1mg IV q3h PRN
Ketorolac 15-30mg IV q6h
Ondansetron 4mg IV q6h PRN
Imipenem/meropenem if infected necrosis
ddx: Peptic ulcer perforation, Cholecystitis, Mesenteric ischemia, SBO, MI (inferior), Aortic dissection, DKA
labs:
Lipase (>3x ULN diagnostic)
Amylase (less specific)
CBC, BMP, LFTs, calcium
Triglycerides
Lactate
ABG if severe
RUQ US (gallstones)
CT abdomen with contrast (for complications, not diagnosis)
MRCP if unclear etiology
notes:
Most common causes: Gallstones (40%), Alcohol (30%) - GET SMASHED mnemonic
Bedside Index of Severity (BISAP), Ranson criteria, APACHE II for prognosis
Severe: organ failure persisting >48h
Necrotizing pancreatitis: CT 72h+ after onset
Walled-off necrosis develops at 4+ weeks
Avoid morphine (theoretical sphincter of Oddi spasm - debated)

name: GI Bleed - Upper
signs:
Hematemesis (bright red or coffee-ground)
Melena (black tarry stools)
Hematochezia (if massive)
Hypotension/tachycardia
Orthostatic changes
Abdominal pain/tenderness
Pallor
Dizziness/syncope
treatment:
1. Two large-bore IVs, aggressive resuscitation
2. Type and crossmatch, transfuse PRBCs if Hgb <7 (or <9 if CAD)
3. IV PPI (pantoprazole) bolus + infusion
4. Octreotide if variceal bleed suspected
5. EGD within 24h (12h if high-risk)
6. Erythromycin 250mg IV 30min before EGD (prokinetic)
7. Intubate for airway protection if massive bleed/altered MS
meds:
Pantoprazole 80mg IV bolus then 8mg/h infusion
Octreotide 50mcg IV bolus then 50mcg/h (varices)
Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily (if cirrhosis)
Erythromycin 250mg IV (prokinetic pre-EGD)
Transexamic acid 1g IV (controversial)
PRBC transfusion
ddx: PUD (most common), Variceal bleed, Mallory-Weiss tear, Erosive gastritis, Dieulafoy lesion, AVM, Aortoenteric fistula, Malignancy
labs:
CBC (Hgb may be normal initially)
BMP (BUN:Cr ratio >20 suggests upper)
Coags (PT/INR, PTT)
LFTs
Type and screen/crossmatch
Lactate
ECG
NG lavage (largely abandoned but occasionally useful)
notes:
Glasgow-Blatchford score: 0 = low risk, safe for outpatient
Rockall score: post-endoscopy risk stratification
PPI before endoscopy reduces high-risk stigmata but not mortality
Variceal: band ligation preferred over sclerotherapy
Rebleeding risk highest in first 72h
Aortoenteric fistula: herald bleed then massive - always consider post-aortic surgery

name: Acute Cholecystitis
signs:
RUQ pain (constant, not colicky)
Murphy sign (inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation)
Fever
Nausea/vomiting
RUQ guarding
Palpable gallbladder (rare)
Jaundice (if CBD involvement)
treatment:
1. NPO
2. IV fluids
3. IV antibiotics
4. Cholecystectomy within 72h (early preferred)
5. Percutaneous cholecystostomy if poor surgical candidate
6. ERCP if choledocholithiasis
7. Pain management
meds:
Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h
OR Ceftriaxone 2g IV + Metronidazole 500mg IV
Ketorolac 15-30mg IV
Morphine/hydromorphone PRN
Ondansetron 4mg IV
ddx: Biliary colic, Choledocholithiasis, Cholangitis, Peptic ulcer disease, Pancreatitis, Hepatitis, RLL pneumonia, Fitz-Hugh-Curtis
labs:
CBC (leukocytosis)
CMP (LFTs, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase)
Lipase
RUQ ultrasound (gallstones, wall thickening >3mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy)
HIDA scan if US inconclusive
MRCP if CBD stone suspected
notes:
Charcot triad (cholangitis): RUQ pain, fever, jaundice
Reynolds pentad: add AMS and hypotension (suppurative cholangitis)
Accalculous cholecystitis: 10% of cases, ICU patients, higher mortality
Tokyo Guidelines for severity grading
Choledocholithiasis risk: elevated LFTs + dilated CBD >6mm

name: Small Bowel Obstruction
signs:
Colicky abdominal pain
Nausea/vomiting (bilious, then feculent)
Abdominal distension
Constipation/obstipation
High-pitched/hyperactive bowel sounds
Prior surgical scars
Dehydration
Tachycardia
treatment:
1. NPO
2. IV fluid resuscitation (large volumes may be needed)
3. NG tube decompression
4. Correct electrolytes
5. Serial abdominal exams
6. Surgical consultation
7. Operative management if complete obstruction, strangulation, or failure to improve in 48-72h
8. Water-soluble contrast challenge (Gastrografin) therapeutic and diagnostic
meds:
Lactated Ringer IV resuscitation
Ondansetron 4mg IV q6h
Ketorolac 15-30mg IV (avoid opioids initially if possible)
Gastrografin 100mL via NGT
Antibiotics if peritonitis: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
ddx: Ileus, Large bowel obstruction, Volvulus, Hernia, Crohn stricture, Mesenteric ischemia, Ogilvie syndrome
labs:
CBC (leukocytosis with strangulation)
BMP (dehydration, electrolyte derangement)
Lactate (elevated with strangulation/ischemia)
LFTs
Lipase
Abdominal X-ray (air-fluid levels, dilated loops >3cm)
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (transition point, closed loop)
notes:
Most common cause: adhesions from prior surgery (60-70%)
Closed loop obstruction: surgical emergency (strangulation risk)
Signs of strangulation: fever, tachycardia, peritonitis, leukocytosis, lactate elevation
Complete vs partial: passage of gas/stool
Gastrografin in colon on 24h film predicts resolution without surgery

name: Cirrhosis
signs:
Jaundice
Ascites
Spider angiomata
Palmar erythema
Caput medusae
Gynecomastia
Asterixis (hepatic flap)
Hepatomegaly then shrunken liver
Splenomegaly
Edema
Easy bruising
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause (alcohol cessation, antivirals for HBV/HCV)
2. Ascites: sodium restriction, diuretics, paracentesis
3. Variceal prophylaxis: nonselective beta-blocker or band ligation
4. HE: lactulose, rifaximin
5. HCC screening: US + AFP q6mo
6. Liver transplant evaluation
7. Avoid hepatotoxins (NSAIDs, acetaminophen >2g/day)
meds:
Spironolactone 100mg + Furosemide 40mg daily (100:40 ratio)
Lactulose 15-30mL PO titrate to 2-3 BM/day
Rifaximin 550mg BID
Nadolol 20-40mg daily (variceal prophylaxis)
Albumin 25% for large-volume paracentesis (6-8g per liter removed >5L)
ddx: CHF (cardiac cirrhosis), Budd-Chiari syndrome, Hepatic metastases, Schistosomiasis
labs:
CMP (LFTs, albumin, bilirubin)
CBC (thrombocytopenia from splenism)
PT/INR (synthetic function)
Ammonia level
Hep B/C serologies
Iron studies, ceruloplasmin, A1AT, ANA/ASMA
US with doppler
Fibroscan/MR elastography
Child-Pugh and MELD scores
notes:
Child-Pugh: A (5-6), B (7-9), C (10-15) - encephalopathy, ascites, bilirubin, albumin, INR
MELD: bilirubin, INR, creatinine - used for transplant prioritization
SBP: ascites WBC >250 neutrophils - treat with ceftriaxone
Hepatorenal syndrome: Type 1 (rapid), Type 2 (gradual)
SBP prophylaxis with norfloxacin or TMP-SMX if prior SBP or GI bleed

name: Sepsis / Septic Shock
signs:
Fever or hypothermia
Tachycardia
Tachypnea
Hypotension (MAP <65)
Altered mental status
Warm then cold extremities
Mottled skin
Decreased urine output
Petechiae/purpura (DIC)
treatment:
1. Hour-1 Bundle: lactate, blood cultures, broad-spectrum abx, 30mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive/lactate >=4, vasopressors if persistent hypotension
2. Source control (drain abscess, remove device)
3. Reassess volume status
4. Target MAP >=65
5. Repeat lactate if initially elevated
6. Stress-dose steroids if refractory shock
meds:
Norepinephrine first-line vasopressor 0.1-2mcg/kg/min
Vasopressin 0.04 units/min (second agent)
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h OR Meropenem 1g IV q8h
Vancomycin 25-30mg/kg IV loading
Hydrocortisone 50mg IV q6h (refractory shock)
Lactated Ringer 30mL/kg bolus
ddx: Cardiogenic shock, Hypovolemic shock, Anaphylaxis, Adrenal crisis, Neurogenic shock, Toxic shock syndrome
labs:
Blood cultures x2 (before abx)
Lactate
CBC with diff
BMP, LFTs
Coags (DIC panel)
ProCalcitonin
Urinalysis + culture
CXR
Source-specific imaging
ABG/VBG
notes:
Sepsis-3: suspected infection + SOFA score increase >=2
qSOFA (screening): RR >=22, AMS, SBP <=100
Septic shock: sepsis + vasopressors needed for MAP >=65 + lactate >2 despite adequate fluids
Each hour delay in antibiotics increases mortality ~4%
Dynamic measures (pulse pressure variation, passive leg raise) better than CVP for fluid responsiveness

name: Cellulitis
signs:
Erythema (spreading, poorly demarcated)
Warmth
Swelling/edema
Tenderness/pain
Fever
Lymphadenopathy
Lymphangitis (red streaking)
Systemic symptoms if severe
treatment:
1. Mild (outpatient): oral antibiotics 5-7 days
2. Moderate-severe (IV): parenteral antibiotics
3. Elevate affected extremity
4. Mark borders with pen to track progression
5. Incision and drainage if abscess
6. MRSA coverage if: purulent, abscess, IVDU, prior MRSA, failure of beta-lactam
meds:
Non-purulent: Cephalexin 500mg PO QID or Dicloxacillin 500mg PO QID
Purulent/MRSA risk: TMP-SMX DS BID or Doxycycline 100mg BID
Severe: Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h + Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h
I&D for abscess (antibiotics may not be needed if small, uncomplicated abscess)
ddx: DVT, Erysipelas, Necrotizing fasciitis, Stasis dermatitis, Contact dermatitis, Gout, Insect bite reaction
labs:
Usually clinical diagnosis
Blood cultures if systemic toxicity
CBC, BMP, CRP
US to evaluate for abscess
Wound culture if draining/abscess
Consider MRI/CT if necrotizing fasciitis suspected
notes:
Most common cause: Group A Strep (GAS) and S. aureus
Erysipelas: more superficial, well-demarcated, raised borders
Necrotizing fasciitis red flags: pain out of proportion, crepitus, rapid spread, gray/dusky skin, bullae, systemic toxicity
Recurrent cellulitis: consider prophylactic penicillin VK 250mg BID

name: Urinary Tract Infection / Pyelonephritis
signs:
Dysuria
Frequency
Urgency
Suprapubic pain/pressure
Hematuria
Cloudy/foul-smelling urine
Pyelonephritis adds: Flank pain, CVA tenderness, Fever/chills, Nausea/vomiting, Systemic illness
treatment:
1. Uncomplicated cystitis: short-course antibiotics
2. Complicated UTI/pyelonephritis: longer course, consider IV
3. Admit if: sepsis, unable to tolerate PO, pregnancy, obstruction
4. Remove/replace urinary catheter if present
5. Hydration
meds:
Cystitis: Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x5d or TMP-SMX DS BID x3d
Pyelonephritis (outpatient): Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID x7d or TMP-SMX DS BID x14d
Pyelonephritis (inpatient): Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily or Ciprofloxacin 400mg IV q12h
Pregnancy: Cephalexin 500mg QID x7d or Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x7d (avoid at term)
ddx: Vaginitis, STI (urethritis), Interstitial cystitis, Nephrolithiasis, Prostatitis, Bladder cancer
labs:
Urinalysis (pyuria, bacteriuria, nitrites, leukocyte esterase)
Urine culture (>100k CFU/mL significant)
CBC, BMP if systemic
Blood cultures if sepsis/pyelonephritis
CT abdomen/pelvis if concerned for obstruction or abscess
Renal US in pregnancy
notes:
Most common: E. coli (80% of uncomplicated UTIs)
Complicated: male, pregnant, catheter, structural abnormality, immunocompromised
Asymptomatic bacteriuria: only treat in pregnancy or pre-urologic procedure
Recurrent UTI (>=3/year): consider prophylaxis, cranberry (weak evidence), post-coital prophylaxis
Fluoroquinolone-sparing approach preferred by FDA

name: HIV / AIDS
signs:
Acute retroviral syndrome (2-4wk post-exposure): fever, pharyngitis, rash, lymphadenopathy, myalgias
Chronic: weight loss, oral candidiasis, hairy leukoplakia, generalized lymphadenopathy
AIDS: opportunistic infections, Kaposi sarcoma, wasting, dementia, CD4 <200
treatment:
1. Start ART immediately (same day if possible)
2. ART for all patients regardless of CD4
3. Preferred initial regimen: integrase inhibitor-based
4. OI prophylaxis based on CD4 count
5. Monitor viral load and CD4
6. Screen and treat coinfections (Hep B/C, TB, STIs)
7. PrEP/PEP for exposure prevention
meds:
Biktarvy (bictegravir/emtricitabine/TAF) 1 tab daily
Dolutegravir 50mg + Descovy (TAF/emtricitabine)
OI prophylaxis:
CD4 <200: TMP-SMX DS daily (PCP)
CD4 <100: Azithromycin 1200mg weekly (MAC)
CD4 <50: consider MAC prophylaxis
PEP: Truvada + Dolutegravir x28 days
ddx: Mononucleosis, Acute hepatitis, Secondary syphilis, Drug reaction, SLE, Lymphoma
labs:
HIV-1/2 Ag/Ab combo (4th gen screening)
HIV RNA viral load (confirmatory and monitoring)
CD4 count and percentage
Resistance testing (genotype before starting ART)
CBC, CMP, lipids, A1c
Hep A/B/C, Syphilis RPR, TB (QuantiFERON)
Toxoplasma IgG, CMV IgG
notes:
AIDS: CD4 <200 or AIDS-defining illness
Common OIs by CD4:
<200: PCP, Toxoplasmosis
<100: Cryptococcus, Cryptosporidium
<50: MAC, CMV
IRIS: immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome after starting ART
U=U: Undetectable = Untransmittable
PrEP: Truvada or Descovy for high-risk individuals

name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
signs:
Polyuria/polydipsia
Nausea/vomiting
Abdominal pain
Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid)
Fruity breath odor
Dehydration
Altered mental status
Tachycardia
Hypotension
Weight loss
treatment:
1. Aggressive IV fluids: NS 1-1.5L/h first hour
2. IV insulin infusion 0.1-0.14 units/kg/h
3. Potassium replacement (if K <5.2, add to fluids)
4. Switch to D5 1/2NS when glucose <200
5. Overlap SC insulin 1-2h before stopping drip
6. Bicarbonate only if pH <6.9
7. Monitor q1-2h: glucose, BMP, VBG
meds:
Regular insulin 0.1 units/kg IV bolus then 0.1 units/kg/h
OR 0.14 units/kg/h (no bolus)
NS 1-1.5L/h initially then 250-500mL/h
KCl 20-40mEq per liter of fluid (if K <5.2)
NaHCO3 100mEq in 400mL if pH <6.9
D5 1/2NS when glucose <200
ddx: HHS, Alcoholic ketoacidosis, Starvation ketosis, Lactic acidosis, Uremia, Toxic ingestion (methanol, ethylene glycol)
labs:
BMP (anion gap metabolic acidosis)
Glucose (usually >250)
VBG/ABG (pH <7.3)
Serum ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate >3)
Urinalysis (ketones, glucose)
CBC
Phosphate
Calculate corrected Na: add 1.6 for each 100 glucose >100
Serum osmolality
notes:
DKA triad: hyperglycemia, ketonemia, anion gap metabolic acidosis
Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3), normal 8-12
Do NOT give insulin if K <3.3 - replace K first
Closing the gap: target is AG normalization, not glucose
Euglycemic DKA: can occur with SGLT2 inhibitors - glucose may be near normal
Transition to SC insulin when: glucose <200, AG closed, bicarb >=15, pH >7.3, patient eating

name: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State
signs:
Profound dehydration
Altered mental status/coma
Extreme hyperglycemia (>600)
Polyuria/polydipsia
Weakness
Visual changes
Seizures
Focal neurologic deficits
Tachycardia
Hypotension
treatment:
1. Aggressive IV fluids (NS or 1/2NS depending on Na)
2. Fluid deficit typically 8-10L - replace 50% in first 12h
3. IV insulin (lower rate than DKA: 0.05-0.1 units/kg/h)
4. Potassium replacement
5. Monitor glucose q1h, BMP q2-4h
6. Treat precipitating cause
7. VTE prophylaxis (high risk)
meds:
NS 1-1.5L first hour then adjust by corrected Na
Regular insulin 0.05-0.1 units/kg/h IV
KCl 20-40mEq/L as needed
Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (DVT prophylaxis)
ddx: DKA, Stroke, Sepsis, Drug overdose, Uremia
labs:
Glucose (often >600, can be >1000)
Serum osmolality >320 (calculated: 2Na + Gluc/18 + BUN/2.8)
BMP
CBC
Calculated corrected sodium
Urinalysis
Blood cultures
CXR
No significant ketosis (bicarb >18, pH >7.3)
notes:
More common in T2DM, elderly, nursing home patients
Mortality higher than DKA (10-20% vs 1-5%)
Precipitants: infection, MI, stroke, non-compliance, new diagnosis
Avoid rapid glucose correction (risk of cerebral edema)
Do not use 1/2NS initially if corrected Na is low
Focal deficits may mimic stroke - resolve with treatment

name: Thyroid Storm
signs:
High fever (>104F)
Severe tachycardia/atrial fibrillation
Agitation/psychosis/delirium
Diaphoresis
Nausea/vomiting/diarrhea
Heart failure
Tremor
Seizures
Jaundice
Coma
treatment:
1. PTU (blocks synthesis AND peripheral conversion)
2. Beta-blocker (propranolol preferred - also blocks T4->T3)
3. Iodine (1 hour AFTER PTU to avoid Wolff-Chaikoff escape)
4. Hydrocortisone (blocks T4->T3 conversion, treats adrenal insufficiency)
5. Cooling measures (avoid aspirin - displaces T4 from binding proteins)
6. Treat precipitant
7. ICU admission
meds:
PTU 200mg PO/PR q4h (then switch to methimazole after crisis)
Propranolol 60-80mg PO q4h or Esmolol 500mcg/kg bolus then 50-200mcg/kg/min
Saturated solution of potassium iodide (SSKI) 5 drops q6h (1hr after PTU)
Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h
Acetaminophen for fever (NOT aspirin)
ddx: Sepsis, Neuroleptic malignant syndrome, Malignant hyperthermia, Sympathomimetic toxicity, Pheochromocytoma, Serotonin syndrome
labs:
TSH (suppressed/undetectable)
Free T4 (markedly elevated)
Free T3 (elevated)
CBC, BMP, LFTs (often abnormal)
Calcium
Cortisol
Blood cultures (r/o infection)
ECG
CXR
notes:
Burch-Wartofsky score >=45 = thyroid storm
Order of treatment matters: PTU first, then iodine 1h later
If iodine given before PTU, thyroid uses it to make more hormone
Common precipitants: infection, surgery, trauma, DKA, iodine contrast, medication noncompliance
Mortality 10-30% even with treatment

name: Adrenal Crisis
signs:
Hypotension/shock refractory to fluids and vasopressors
Severe weakness/fatigue
Abdominal pain/nausea/vomiting
Fever
Altered mental status
Hyponatremia
Hyperkalemia
Hypoglycemia
Hyperpigmentation (chronic primary AI)
treatment:
1. IV hydrocortisone 100mg STAT (do not delay for diagnosis)
2. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with NS
3. Correct hypoglycemia with D50
4. Treat precipitating illness
5. Transition to oral steroids when stable
6. Mineralocorticoid replacement not needed acutely (hydrocortisone has mineralocorticoid activity at high doses)
meds:
Hydrocortisone 100mg IV bolus then 50mg IV q8h
NS 1-2L rapid bolus then continue resuscitation
D50 if hypoglycemic
Fludrocortisone 0.1mg PO daily (when eating, for primary AI)
ddx: Septic shock, MI, PE, Meningococcemia, Thyroid storm, Opioid overdose
labs:
Cortisol level (draw before giving steroids if possible, but do NOT delay treatment)
ACTH level (high in primary, low in secondary)
BMP (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia)
Glucose
CBC
TSH
Cosyntropin stimulation test (when stable)
notes:
Triggers: infection, surgery, trauma, abrupt steroid withdrawal
Patients on chronic steroids need stress-dose steroids for illness/surgery
Stress dosing: Hydrocortisone 50mg IV q8h for moderate illness, 100mg q8h for severe
Waterhouse-Friderichsen: bilateral adrenal hemorrhage from meningococcemia
Do NOT delay treatment for lab confirmation

name: Acute Kidney Injury
signs:
Oliguria or anuria
Fluid overload (edema, pulmonary congestion)
Fatigue/malaise
Nausea/vomiting
Confusion (uremia)
Pruritus
Asterixis (severe)
Pericardial friction rub (uremia)
Metallic taste
treatment:
1. Identify and treat cause (prerenal, intrinsic, postrenal)
2. Prerenal: IV fluid resuscitation
3. Postrenal: relieve obstruction (Foley, nephrostomy)
4. Intrinsic: treat underlying (stop nephrotoxins, treat GN)
5. Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, contrast, aminoglycosides)
6. Adjust renally-dosed medications
7. Dialysis indications: AEIOU
meds:
NS or LR for prerenal (fluid resuscitation)
Furosemide 40-80mg IV (if fluid overloaded, NOT to treat AKI)
Calcium gluconate 1g IV (if hyperkalemia with ECG changes)
Regular insulin 10 units + D50 (shift K intracellularly)
Sodium bicarbonate 150mEq in D5W for acidosis
Kayexalate 15-30g PO (controversial)
ddx: CKD, Prerenal azotemia, ATN, AIN, Glomerulonephritis, Obstructive uropathy, Hepatorenal syndrome, Rhabdomyolysis
labs:
BMP (creatinine trend, BUN, K, bicarb)
Urinalysis with microscopy (casts: muddy brown = ATN, WBC = AIN, RBC = GN)
FENa (<1% prerenal, >2% intrinsic) or FEUrea if on diuretics
CBC
Renal ultrasound (hydronephrosis, kidney size)
Urine electrolytes
CK (rhabdomyolysis)
notes:
KDIGO staging: Stage 1 (1.5-1.9x), Stage 2 (2-2.9x), Stage 3 (>=3x or Cr >=4 or dialysis)
Dialysis indications (AEIOU): Acidosis, Electrolytes (K), Ingestions, Overload, Uremia symptoms
FENa = (UNa x PCr)/(PNa x UCr) x 100
Prerenal: BUN:Cr >20, FENa <1%, bland sediment
ATN: muddy brown casts, FENa >2%
Contrast nephropathy prevention: IV hydration pre/post, hold metformin

name: Hyperkalemia
signs:
Often asymptomatic until severe
Muscle weakness (ascending)
Paresthesias
Palpitations
Bradycardia
Nausea
Paralysis (severe)
Cardiac arrest (severe)
ECG changes: peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave
treatment:
1. Stabilize myocardium: calcium gluconate (if ECG changes)
2. Shift K intracellularly: insulin + glucose, albuterol, bicarb
3. Remove K from body: diuretics, kayexalate/patiromer, dialysis
4. Continuous cardiac monitoring
5. Treat underlying cause
6. Stop offending medications
meds:
Calcium gluconate 1-2g IV over 2-5min (cardioprotection, onset 1-3min)
Regular insulin 10 units IV + D50 25g IV (shift, onset 15-30min)
Albuterol 10-20mg nebulized (shift)
Furosemide 40-80mg IV (removal)
Sodium polystyrene (Kayexalate) 15-30g PO (removal, slow)
Patiromer 8.4g PO (newer, better tolerated)
Sodium bicarbonate 50-100mEq IV (if acidotic)
ddx: Pseudohyperkalemia (hemolyzed sample, tourniquet), AKI/CKD, Rhabdomyolysis, Adrenal insufficiency, Medications (ACEi, K-sparing diuretics, TMP-SMX), Tumor lysis, Acidosis
labs:
BMP (K level, renal function)
ECG (peaked T -> prolonged PR -> wide QRS -> sine wave -> VF)
VBG/ABG (acidosis)
Repeat K to confirm
CBC
CK (rhabdomyolysis)
Digoxin level if applicable
Urine K, TTKG
notes:
ECG changes roughly correlate: >5.5: peaked T; >6.5: loss of P wave, wide QRS; >7: sine wave, VF
Calcium does NOT lower K - it stabilizes the myocardium
Always give D50 with insulin to prevent hypoglycemia
Check glucose q1h x4h after insulin
Dialysis is definitive treatment for severe/refractory
ACEi/ARB + K-sparing diuretic + TMP-SMX = triple whammy for hyperK

name: Hip Fracture
signs:
Hip pain after fall
Shortened and externally rotated leg
Inability to bear weight
Pain with axial loading
Pain with log roll
Ecchymosis (delayed)
Groin pain
treatment:
1. Operative repair within 24-48h
2. Femoral neck: hemiarthroplasty (elderly, displaced) or ORIF (young, non-displaced)
3. Intertrochanteric: ORIF with sliding hip screw or IM nail
4. Pain management (femoral nerve block)
5. DVT prophylaxis
6. Early mobilization
7. Osteoporosis workup and treatment
meds:
Fascia iliaca block or femoral nerve block
Acetaminophen 1g IV/PO q6h
Oxycodone 5-10mg PO q4h PRN
Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (DVT prophylaxis)
Calcium + Vitamin D
Bisphosphonate (after fracture healed)
ddx: Pelvic fracture, Pubic ramus fracture, Greater trochanteric bursitis, Pathologic fracture, Hip dislocation, Stress fracture
labs:
X-ray pelvis AP + lateral hip
MRI if X-ray negative but high suspicion
CBC, BMP, coags
Type and screen
ECG (preop)
Vitamin D level
DEXA scan (osteoporosis)
notes:
Femoral neck fractures at risk for AVN (blood supply disruption)
Garden classification for femoral neck displacement
Evans classification for intertrochanteric
Mortality: 20-30% at 1 year in elderly
Delaying surgery >48h increases complications and mortality
Occult hip fracture: normal X-ray in 2-10% - get MRI

name: Compartment Syndrome
signs:
Pain out of proportion to injury
Pain with passive stretch (earliest reliable sign)
Paresthesias
Pressure/tenseness of compartment
Pallor (late)
Pulselessness (very late)
Paralysis (late - indicates irreversible damage)
treatment:
1. EMERGENT fasciotomy (definitive treatment)
2. Remove all constrictive dressings/casts
3. Keep limb at heart level (NOT elevated)
4. Compartment pressure measurement if diagnosis uncertain
5. Do NOT delay fasciotomy for imaging
6. IV fluids to prevent rhabdomyolysis/AKI
meds:
IV fluids (NS or LR aggressive hydration)
Analgesia: morphine/hydromorphone IV (inadequate pain relief is a clue)
Sodium bicarbonate (if rhabdomyolysis)
Mannitol (sometimes used for muscle swelling)
ddx: Fracture, DVT, Cellulitis, Necrotizing fasciitis, Rhabdomyolysis without compartment syndrome, Peripheral arterial occlusion
labs:
Compartment pressure measurement (>30mmHg or within 30mmHg of diastolic = fasciotomy)
CK (rhabdomyolysis)
BMP (K, creatinine)
Urinalysis (myoglobinuria)
CBC
Lactate
X-ray of extremity
notes:
6 Ps: Pain, Pressure, Paresthesias, Paralysis, Pallor, Pulselessness
Pulselessness is a LATE finding - do not wait for it
Anterior compartment of leg most commonly affected
Causes: fractures (tibia most common), crush injury, tight casts, reperfusion after vascular injury
Irreversible damage begins at 6-8h
Clinical diagnosis - do not delay for pressure measurement if high suspicion

name: Anaphylaxis
signs:
Urticaria/angioedema
Dyspnea/wheezing/stridor
Hypotension
Tachycardia
Abdominal pain/nausea/vomiting
Dizziness/syncope
Sense of doom
Pruritus
Flushed skin
Rapid onset after exposure
treatment:
1. Epinephrine IM (anterolateral thigh) IMMEDIATELY
2. Remove trigger if possible
3. Supine position with legs elevated (unless respiratory distress)
4. IV fluids for hypotension
5. Adjuncts: antihistamines, steroids, bronchodilators
6. Observe 4-6h minimum (biphasic reaction risk)
7. Prescribe epinephrine autoinjector at discharge
meds:
Epinephrine 0.3-0.5mg (1:1000) IM q5-15min as needed
Epinephrine infusion 0.1-0.5mcg/kg/min if refractory
Diphenhydramine 50mg IV
Famotidine 20mg IV
Methylprednisolone 125mg IV
Albuterol 2.5mg nebulized (bronchospasm)
NS 1-2L rapid bolus
ddx: Asthma exacerbation, Vasovagal syncope, Angioedema (ACEi), Carcinoid syndrome, Mastocytosis, Panic attack, Vocal cord dysfunction
labs:
Clinical diagnosis - do NOT delay treatment for labs
Tryptase (draw within 1-2h, peaks at 1h, repeat at 24h as baseline)
CBC
BMP
ABG if severe respiratory distress
notes:
Epinephrine is THE treatment - there is NO contraindication in anaphylaxis
IM (vastus lateralis) not SC - faster absorption
Biphasic reaction in up to 20% - occurs 1-72h after initial reaction (usually 8-10h)
Common triggers: foods (nuts, shellfish), medications (penicillin, NSAIDs), insect stings, latex
Do NOT rely on antihistamines/steroids alone - they do not treat the life-threatening components

name: Acetaminophen Overdose
signs:
Phase 1 (0-24h): Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, malaise
Phase 2 (24-72h): RUQ pain, elevated LFTs, improving nausea
Phase 3 (72-96h): Hepatic failure, jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, renal failure
Phase 4 (4-14d): Recovery or death
treatment:
1. Activated charcoal if <4h since ingestion
2. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) - most effective within 8h but give if any concern
3. Use Rumack-Matthew nomogram for acute single ingestion
4. Check APAP level at 4h post-ingestion
5. Liver transplant evaluation if fulminant failure
6. King College Criteria for transplant listing
meds:
Activated charcoal 1g/kg PO (if <4h)
NAC IV protocol: 150mg/kg over 1h, then 50mg/kg over 4h, then 100mg/kg over 16h
NAC PO: 140mg/kg loading, then 70mg/kg q4h x17 doses
Ondansetron 4mg IV PRN (NAC causes nausea)
ddx: Viral hepatitis, Alcoholic hepatitis, Mushroom poisoning (Amanita), Drug-induced liver injury, Wilson disease, Budd-Chiari
labs:
APAP level at 4h post-ingestion (plot on Rumack-Matthew)
AST/ALT (may be normal initially)
PT/INR (best marker of synthetic function)
BMP (creatinine, bicarb)
Lipase
Lactate
Phosphate (low = poor prognosis)
Blood gas (pH <7.3 = poor prognosis)
notes:
Toxic dose: >150mg/kg or >7.5g in adults
NAC is nearly 100% effective if given within 8h
Rumack-Matthew nomogram: plot 4h level vs time - treat if above treatment line
Kings College (non-APAP ALF): INR >6.5, or 3 of: age <10 or >40, non-A non-B, jaundice >7d before encephalopathy, INR >3.5, bilirubin >17.5
Chronic APAP: nomogram may not apply, lower threshold to treat

name: Alcohol Withdrawal
signs:
Tremor (6-24h)
Anxiety/agitation
Insomnia
Nausea/vomiting
Diaphoresis
Tachycardia/HTN
Seizures (12-48h)
Hallucinations (12-48h, often visual)
Delirium tremens (48-96h): confusion, agitation, autonomic instability, fever
treatment:
1. Benzodiazepines (symptom-triggered preferred over fixed-dose)
2. CIWA-Ar protocol for monitoring
3. Thiamine 500mg IV x3 days BEFORE glucose
4. Folate 1mg IV/PO daily
5. Fluid/electrolyte replacement
6. ICU for DTs or severe withdrawal
7. Phenobarbital or propofol for benzo-resistant cases
meds:
Diazepam 10-20mg IV q5-10min (symptom-triggered)
OR Lorazepam 2-4mg IV q15-20min (hepatic impairment)
Chlordiazepoxide 50-100mg PO q6h (mild, taper)
Phenobarbital 260mg IV (benzo-resistant)
Thiamine 500mg IV TID x3d then 100mg PO daily
Folate 1mg daily
MgSO4 2g IV if hypomagnesemic
ddx: Thyroid storm, Sympathomimetic toxicity, Anticholinergic toxicity, Serotonin syndrome, NMS, Sepsis, Meningitis, Hepatic encephalopathy
labs:
BAL
CBC, BMP (Mg, Phos, Ca)
LFTs
Lipase
Ammonia
Coags
Blood gas
Urinalysis
CXR
CT head if altered or seizures or trauma
notes:
DTs mortality 5-15% untreated, <1% with treatment
CIWA-Ar score: >=10 treat, >=20 severe
Seizures: typically generalized tonic-clonic, brief, self-limited
Kindling: repeated withdrawal episodes become progressively more severe
Always give thiamine BEFORE glucose (prevent Wernicke)
Wernicke triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia (all 3 present in only 10%)

name: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
signs:
Simultaneous bleeding AND clotting
Petechiae/purpura
Oozing from IV sites, wounds
Mucosal bleeding
Hematuria
GI bleeding
Thromboembolic events
Organ dysfunction
Acral ischemia/gangrene
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause (this is key)
2. Transfuse based on clinical bleeding, not just lab values
3. Platelets if <10k (or <50k with active bleeding)
4. FFP for prolonged PT/INR with bleeding
5. Cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <150
6. Heparin only in selected cases (predominant thrombosis)
7. Do NOT give antifibrinolytics in acute DIC
meds:
Platelets: 1 unit per 10kg
FFP: 15mL/kg
Cryoprecipitate: 10 units (for fibrinogen)
PRBCs as needed
Heparin (only if thrombosis-predominant): 500-750 units/h without bolus
ddx: TTP, HUS, HELLP syndrome, Liver failure (coagulopathy), Vitamin K deficiency, Primary fibrinolysis, ITP
labs:
CBC (thrombocytopenia, MAHA on smear)
PT/INR (prolonged)
PTT (prolonged)
Fibrinogen (low, <150)
D-dimer (markedly elevated)
Peripheral smear (schistocytes)
LDH (elevated)
Haptoglobin (decreased)
ISSC DIC score
notes:
DIC is always secondary to an underlying condition
Common triggers: sepsis, trauma, obstetric emergencies, malignancy (esp APL), snake envenomation
Acute DIC: bleeding predominant; Chronic DIC: thrombosis predominant
APL (Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia): DIC is hallmark - treat with ATRA
ISSC score >=5 = overt DIC

name: Sickle Cell Crisis
signs:
Severe pain (bones, chest, abdomen)
Fever
Swelling of hands/feet (dactylitis in children)
Acute chest syndrome: fever, chest pain, infiltrate, hypoxia
Stroke symptoms
Priapism
Splenic sequestration (LUQ pain, splenomegaly, acute anemia)
Jaundice
treatment:
1. Aggressive pain management (do not undertreat)
2. IV fluids (avoid overhydration)
3. Oxygen if hypoxic
4. Acute chest syndrome: antibiotics + exchange transfusion
5. Simple transfusion for severe anemia (Hgb <5 or symptomatic)
6. Exchange transfusion for stroke, severe ACS
7. Incentive spirometry (prevent ACS)
8. Hydroxyurea for prevention
meds:
Morphine 0.1-0.15mg/kg IV q2-3h or PCA
Ketorolac 15-30mg IV q6h
Acetaminophen 1g PO q6h
IV NS maintenance (avoid boluses unless dehydrated)
Ceftriaxone 2g IV + Azithromycin 500mg (ACS)
Hydroxyurea 15-35mg/kg/day (chronic, prevention)
Folic acid 1mg daily
ddx: Osteomyelitis, Avascular necrosis, PE, Pneumonia, Cholecystitis, Aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19), Splenic sequestration
labs:
CBC with retic count
Reticulocyte count (high in crisis, low in aplastic crisis)
Peripheral smear (sickle cells)
Type and screen
BMP, LFTs, LDH
Bilirubin (unconjugated elevation)
Hgb electrophoresis (if not previously done)
CXR (ACS)
Blood cultures if febrile
Transcranial Doppler (stroke screening in children)
notes:
ACS: leading cause of death in SCD adults - new infiltrate + fever/resp symptoms
Stroke: 11% of SCD patients by age 20, exchange transfusion emergently
Parvovirus B19: causes aplastic crisis (reticulocyte count drops)
Splenic sequestration: acute drop in Hgb >=2 with acute splenic enlargement
Avoid: cold exposure, dehydration, high altitude, overexertion
Screen with transcranial Doppler annually ages 2-16

name: Cardiac Tamponade
signs:
Beck triad: hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds
Tachycardia
Pulsus paradoxus >10mmHg
Dyspnea
Electrical alternans on ECG
Narrow pulse pressure
Shock
treatment:
1. Emergent pericardiocentesis
2. IV fluid bolus to increase preload
3. Avoid diuretics, vasodilators, positive pressure ventilation
4. Surgical pericardial window for recurrent
5. Treat underlying cause
meds:
NS 500mL-1L rapid bolus
Pericardiocentesis: subxiphoid approach, US-guided
Dobutamine if needed post-drainage
Treat cause-specific (antibiotics, chemo, etc)
ddx: Tension pneumothorax, Massive PE, Cardiogenic shock, Constrictive pericarditis, RV infarct
labs:
Bedside echo (pericardial effusion, diastolic RA/RV collapse, IVC plethora)
ECG (low voltage, electrical alternans, sinus tach)
CXR (water-bottle heart)
Pericardial fluid analysis: cell count, protein, LDH, glucose, cytology, culture, ADA
notes:
Even small rapid effusions can cause tamponade
Slow effusions (chronic) can accumulate 1-2L before tamponade
Pulsus paradoxus: >10mmHg drop in SBP with inspiration
Equalization of diastolic pressures on right heart cath
Causes: malignancy, uremia, infection, post-MI (Dressler), trauma, aortic dissection

name: Acute Coronary Syndrome - NSTEMI
signs:
Chest pain/pressure (may be atypical)
Dyspnea
Diaphoresis
Nausea
Pain at rest or with minimal exertion
ST depression or T-wave inversion on ECG
Elevated troponin
treatment:
1. Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor)
2. Anticoagulation (heparin)
3. Risk stratify with TIMI or GRACE score
4. Early invasive strategy (cath within 24h) for high-risk
5. Beta-blocker if no contraindications
6. High-intensity statin
7. Nitrates for ongoing ischemia
meds:
Aspirin 325mg then 81mg daily
Ticagrelor 180mg then 90mg BID (preferred) or Clopidogrel 600mg then 75mg daily
Heparin 60u/kg bolus then 12u/kg/h (PTT 50-70)
Metoprolol 25mg PO
Atorvastatin 80mg PO
Nitroglycerin 0.4mg SL q5min x3 or IV infusion
ddx: STEMI, Unstable angina, PE, Aortic dissection, Myocarditis, Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, Demand ischemia
labs:
Troponin (serial q3-6h, high-sensitivity preferred)
12-lead ECG (serial)
CBC, BMP, lipids
BNP
CXR
Echo
Coronary angiography
notes:
NSTEMI = elevated troponin WITHOUT ST elevation
Unstable angina = ischemic symptoms without troponin elevation
TIMI risk score: age, risk factors, known CAD, ASA use, angina, ST changes, troponin
High-sensitivity troponin: 0/1h or 0/3h rule-out algorithms
Do NOT give fibrinolytics for NSTEMI

name: Pleural Effusion
signs:
Dyspnea
Pleuritic chest pain
Decreased breath sounds
Dullness to percussion
Decreased tactile fremitus
Egophony at fluid level
Tracheal deviation (massive)
Comfort sitting upright
treatment:
1. Thoracentesis (diagnostic and/or therapeutic)
2. Light criteria to classify transudative vs exudative
3. Treat underlying cause
4. Chest tube for empyema or complicated parapneumonic
5. Pleurodesis for recurrent malignant effusion
6. Indwelling pleural catheter for recurrent effusions
meds:
Treat underlying cause:
CHF: diuretics
Infection: antibiotics
Intrapleural tPA 10mg + DNase 5mg BID (complicated parapneumonic)
Pleurodesis: talc slurry via chest tube
ddx:
Transudative: CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome
Exudative: pneumonia, malignancy, PE, TB, rheumatoid, pancreatitis
labs:
Thoracentesis: protein, LDH, glucose, cell count, pH, Gram stain, culture, cytology
Light criteria (exudate if ANY): pleural protein/serum protein >0.5, pleural LDH/serum LDH >0.6, pleural LDH >2/3 upper normal serum
CXR (meniscus sign, >200mL needed on upright)
US-guided thoracentesis recommended
CT chest for complex effusions
notes:
Light criteria: highly sensitive for exudates but can misclassify transudates on diuretics
If borderline, check albumin gradient (serum-pleural >1.2 = transudate)
pH <7.2 or glucose <60 in parapneumonic = chest tube needed
Bloody: malignancy, PE, trauma; Milky: chylothorax (triglycerides >110)
Up to 1.5L can be removed at once (risk of re-expansion pulmonary edema)

name: Pulmonary Fibrosis / ILD
signs:
Progressive dyspnea on exertion
Dry nonproductive cough
Bibasilar inspiratory crackles (velcro)
Clubbing
Cyanosis (late)
Exercise desaturation
Weight loss
Fatigue
treatment:
1. IPF: antifibrotics (pirfenidone or nintedanib)
2. Non-IPF ILD: immunosuppression based on type
3. Supplemental O2 if hypoxemic
4. Pulmonary rehabilitation
5. Lung transplant evaluation for progressive disease
6. Treat GERD (risk factor for IPF progression)
7. Vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID)
meds:
Pirfenidone 801mg TID with food (IPF)
Nintedanib 150mg BID with food (IPF)
Prednisone 0.5-1mg/kg for non-IPF inflammatory ILD
Mycophenolate 1-1.5g BID (connective tissue ILD)
Omeprazole 20mg BID (GERD in IPF)
ddx: COPD, CHF, Asbestosis, Hypersensitivity pneumonitis, Sarcoidosis, Drug-induced ILD, Radiation pneumonitis
labs:
PFTs (restrictive: reduced FVC and TLC, low DLCO)
HRCT chest (UIP pattern: basilar, peripheral honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis)
6-minute walk test (desaturation)
ANA, RF, CCP, myositis panel
BMP
CBC
KL-6, SP-D (biomarkers)
Surgical lung biopsy if HRCT non-diagnostic
notes:
UIP pattern on HRCT is sufficient for IPF diagnosis without biopsy
IPF median survival 3-5 years from diagnosis
Acute exacerbation of IPF: rapid deterioration, bilateral GGO, high mortality
Avoid: smoking, occupational exposures
Nintedanib: GI side effects common; Pirfenidone: photosensitivity
ILD-GAP index for prognosis

name: Guillain-Barre Syndrome
signs:
Ascending symmetric weakness (legs first)
Areflexia/hyporeflexia
Paresthesias (often precede weakness)
Back pain
Autonomic dysfunction (HR, BP lability)
Respiratory failure (25%)
Facial weakness (bilateral)
Difficulty swallowing
treatment:
1. IVIG or plasmapheresis (equally effective)
2. Monitor respiratory function closely (FVC q4-6h)
3. Intubate if FVC <20mL/kg or NIF <-30 cmH2O (20/30/40 rule)
4. DVT prophylaxis
5. Pain management
6. ICU monitoring for autonomic instability
7. Steroids NOT effective
meds:
IVIG 0.4g/kg/day x5 days
OR Plasmapheresis 5 exchanges over 2 weeks
Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (DVT prophylaxis)
Gabapentin 300-600mg TID (neuropathic pain)
Nifedipine or labetalol (autonomic BP swings)
ddx: Transverse myelitis, Myasthenia gravis, Botulism, Tick paralysis, Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP), Spinal cord compression, Poliomyelitis
labs:
LP: albuminocytologic dissociation (high protein, normal WBC) - may be normal first week
Nerve conduction studies/EMG (demyelination pattern)
FVC and NIF serial measurements
Anti-ganglioside antibodies (GQ1b in Miller Fisher)
MRI spine (r/o cord compression, nerve root enhancement)
CBC, BMP
notes:
Typically 1-4 weeks after respiratory/GI illness (Campylobacter jejuni most common)
20/30/40 rule for intubation: FVC <20mL/kg, NIF <-30, >40% decline from baseline
Miller Fisher variant: ataxia, areflexia, ophthalmoplegia
Nadir typically 2-4 weeks; recovery over weeks-months
80% recover fully; 5% mortality
Do NOT give steroids - no benefit and may worsen

name: Myasthenia Gravis
signs:
Fluctuating weakness worse with activity
Ptosis (often first symptom)
Diplopia
Dysphagia
Dysarthria
Facial weakness
Proximal limb weakness
Respiratory weakness (myasthenic crisis)
Improves with rest
treatment:
1. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (pyridostigmine)
2. Immunosuppression for generalized disease
3. Myasthenic crisis: IVIG or plasmapheresis + ICU
4. Thymectomy (if thymoma or generalized anti-AChR+)
5. Avoid myasthenia-worsening drugs
6. Monitor respiratory function
meds:
Pyridostigmine 60mg PO TID (titrate up)
Prednisone 1mg/kg/day (slow taper)
Azathioprine 2-3mg/kg/day
Mycophenolate 1-1.5g BID
IVIG 2g/kg over 5 days (crisis)
Plasmapheresis 5 exchanges (crisis)
Rituximab (refractory)
ddx: Lambert-Eaton syndrome, Botulism, GBS, ALS, Brainstem stroke, MS, Thyroid ophthalmopathy
labs:
Anti-AChR antibodies (85% sensitivity)
Anti-MuSK antibodies (if AChR negative)
CT chest (thymoma)
Edrophonium (Tensilon) test (rarely used now)
Ice pack test (ptosis improves)
Repetitive nerve stimulation (decremental response)
Single-fiber EMG (most sensitive)
PFTs, NIF (respiratory monitoring)
notes:
Drugs that worsen MG: aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, magnesium, phenytoin, lithium, D-penicillamine, immune checkpoint inhibitors
Myasthenic crisis: respiratory failure requiring intubation
Cholinergic crisis: overdose of anticholinesterase (SLUDGE symptoms)
Distinguish with edrophonium or clinical context
Thymoma present in 10-15%; thymectomy even without thymoma if generalized anti-AChR+

name: Inflammatory Bowel Disease - Crohn
signs:
Abdominal pain (often RLQ)
Diarrhea (may be bloody or non-bloody)
Weight loss
Fever
Fatigue
Perianal disease (fistulas, abscesses, tags)
Oral aphthous ulcers
Skip lesions
Bowel obstruction symptoms
treatment:
1. Induction: corticosteroids or biologics
2. Mild: budesonide or mesalamine
3. Moderate-severe: biologics (anti-TNF, anti-integrin, anti-IL12/23)
4. Immunomodulators for maintenance
5. Surgery for complications (stricture, fistula, abscess)
6. Nutritional support
7. Smoking cessation (worsens Crohn)
meds:
Budesonide 9mg PO daily (ileal/right colon)
Prednisone 40-60mg daily taper (flare)
Infliximab 5mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6wk then q8wk
Adalimumab 160mg then 80mg then 40mg SC q2wk
Vedolizumab 300mg IV
Ustekinumab 6mg/kg IV then 90mg SC q8wk
Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg daily
Metronidazole 500mg TID (perianal)
ddx: Ulcerative colitis, Infectious colitis, IBS, Celiac disease, Appendicitis, Intestinal TB, Lymphoma, Diverticulitis
labs:
CBC (anemia, leukocytosis)
CRP, ESR (inflammation markers)
Albumin (nutritional status)
Fecal calprotectin
Stool studies (r/o C. diff, infections)
Colonoscopy with biopsies (cobblestoning, skip lesions, noncaseating granulomas)
CT/MR enterography (strictures, fistulas, abscesses)
Small bowel series or capsule endoscopy
notes:
Crohn vs UC: Crohn = mouth to anus, transmural, skip lesions, granulomas, fistulas
UC = colon only, continuous, mucosal, no granulomas
Extraintestinal: arthritis, erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, uveitis, sclerosing cholangitis (more UC), sacroiliitis
Stricturing vs penetrating vs inflammatory phenotype
Never give anti-TNF without ruling out TB and Hep B first

name: GI Bleed - Lower
signs:
Hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum)
Maroon stools
Abdominal pain/cramping (variable)
Hemodynamic instability if severe
Dizziness/syncope
Pallor
Rectal mass on exam (possible)
treatment:
1. Resuscitation: IV fluids, blood transfusion if needed
2. Identify source: colonoscopy (after prep, within 24h)
3. CT angiography if brisk bleeding
4. Tagged RBC scan for intermittent/slow bleeding
5. Angiographic embolization if ongoing and source identified
6. Surgical consultation for massive uncontrolled bleeding
7. Reverse anticoagulation if applicable
meds:
NS/LR resuscitation
PRBCs: transfuse if Hgb <7 (or <9 with CAD)
Reverse anticoagulation as appropriate
GoLytely 1L/h x4h (colonoscopy prep)
Aminocaproic acid (rare, selected cases)
ddx: Diverticular bleed (most common), Angiodysplasia, Hemorrhoids, Colorectal cancer, IBD, Ischemic colitis, Radiation proctitis, Meckel diverticulum (young), Upper GI bleed (brisk)
labs:
CBC (may be normal initially)
BMP
Coags, type and crossmatch
Lactate
Colonoscopy (after prep)
CT angiography (active extravasation)
Tagged RBC scan
Consider upper endoscopy to rule out brisk upper source
notes:
Diverticular bleeding: painless, self-limited in 80%
Angiodysplasia: elderly, often right colon, associated with aortic stenosis
Always consider upper GI source - 10-15% of hematochezia is from upper GI bleed
Massive transfusion protocol: 1:1:1 ratio PRBCs:FFP:Platelets
Post-polypectomy bleeding: can occur up to 2 weeks after

name: Myxedema Coma
signs:
Hypothermia
Altered mental status/coma
Bradycardia
Hypotension
Hyponatremia
Hypoglycemia
Hypoventilation/respiratory failure
Non-pitting edema (myxedema)
Delayed relaxation of reflexes
Seizures
treatment:
1. IV levothyroxine loading dose
2. IV hydrocortisone (before or with T4 - rule out concurrent adrenal insufficiency)
3. Passive rewarming (avoid active - can cause vasodilation/shock)
4. Mechanical ventilation if needed
5. IV fluids with dextrose cautiously
6. Treat precipitating cause
7. ICU admission
meds:
Levothyroxine 200-400mcg IV loading then 50-100mcg IV daily
Liothyronine (T3) 5-20mcg IV q8h (controversial, some centers add)
Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h (until adrenal insufficiency excluded)
D50 for hypoglycemia
NS for hyponatremia (fluid restrict if severe)
ddx: Sepsis, Stroke, Drug overdose (sedatives, opioids), Hypothermia (primary), Hypoglycemia, Adrenal crisis
labs:
TSH (markedly elevated in primary)
Free T4 (very low)
Free T3
Cortisol (rule out adrenal insufficiency)
BMP (hyponatremia, hypoglycemia)
CBC
ABG/VBG (hypercapnia, hypoxia)
CK (elevated from myopathy)
Lipase, LFTs
notes:
Mortality 30-60% even with treatment
Precipitants: infection, cold exposure, medications (sedatives, amiodarone, lithium), noncompliance, surgery
Give hydrocortisone empirically - cannot distinguish primary from secondary hypothyroidism acutely
Do NOT actively warm (causes peripheral vasodilation and shock)
Hyponatremia from SIADH and impaired free water excretion

name: Gout
signs:
Acute monoarticular arthritis (1st MTP classic - podagra)
Severe pain, swelling, erythema, warmth
Fever
Tophi (chronic)
Joint stiffness
Desquamation over joint after attack
Polyarticular in advanced disease
treatment:
1. Acute flare: NSAIDs, colchicine, or corticosteroids
2. Start within 24h of flare onset
3. Do NOT start or stop allopurinol during acute flare
4. Urate-lowering therapy if: >=2 flares/year, tophi, urate nephropathy, renal stones
5. Treat to target: serum urate <6mg/dL
6. Lifestyle: limit alcohol/purines, weight loss, hydration
meds:
Acute: Indomethacin 50mg TID x5-7d or Naproxen 500mg BID
Colchicine 1.2mg then 0.6mg 1h later (within 36h of onset)
Prednisone 30-40mg daily x5d (if NSAIDs contraindicated)
Intra-articular triamcinolone 10-40mg
Prophylaxis: Allopurinol 100mg daily (titrate to urate <6)
Febuxostat 40-80mg daily
Colchicine 0.6mg daily during ULT initiation
ddx: Septic arthritis, Pseudogout (CPPD), Cellulitis, Rheumatoid arthritis, Reactive arthritis, Trauma
labs:
Joint aspiration (gold standard): negatively birefringent needle-shaped MSU crystals
Cell count (10,000-70,000 WBC, neutrophil predominant)
Gram stain/culture (rule out septic arthritis)
Serum uric acid (may be normal during acute flare)
BMP (renal function)
CBC, ESR/CRP
Joint X-ray (chronic: punched-out erosions, overhanging edges)
Dual-energy CT (advanced)
notes:
Serum urate often NORMAL during acute flare - do not use to rule out gout
Joint aspiration is gold standard but clinical diagnosis acceptable
Allopurinol: start low (100mg), go slow, titrate q2-4wks to target urate <6
Allopurinol flare prophylaxis: colchicine 0.6mg daily for 3-6 months
HLA-B*5801 testing in Southeast Asian, African American patients before allopurinol (SJS risk)

name: Septic Arthritis
signs:
Acute monoarticular arthritis (knee most common)
Severe pain with any movement
Joint effusion
Warmth and erythema
Fever
Refusal to bear weight or move joint
Systemic illness
treatment:
1. Joint aspiration BEFORE antibiotics (but do not delay if septic)
2. Empiric IV antibiotics
3. Surgical drainage/washout (especially hip, prosthetic joints)
4. Serial aspirations or arthroscopic washout
5. Orthopedic/surgical consultation
6. Duration: typically 2-4 weeks IV (varies)
meds:
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h (empiric, covers MRSA)
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily (add for gram-negative coverage)
Nafcillin 2g IV q4h (if MSSA confirmed)
Ceftazidime 2g IV q8h (if Pseudomonas suspected - IVDU)
Gonococcal: Ceftriaxone 1g IV/IM daily
ddx: Gout, Pseudogout, Reactive arthritis, Rheumatoid arthritis flare, Lyme arthritis, Trauma with hemarthrosis, Osteomyelitis
labs:
Joint aspiration (MANDATORY): cell count >50,000 WBC (usually >100k), >75% PMNs
Gram stain (positive in 50-75%)
Synovial fluid culture
Blood cultures x2
CBC, ESR, CRP
BMP
Gonococcal NAAT (urethral, cervical, pharyngeal, rectal)
Joint X-ray (baseline)
MRI (if concern for adjacent osteomyelitis)
notes:
Most common: S. aureus (adults), N. gonorrhoeae (young sexually active adults)
Gonococcal: often migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, dermatitis before settling in one joint
Prosthetic joint infection: lower WBC threshold (>1,100)
Synovial WBC >50,000 with >75% PMNs = septic until proven otherwise
Do NOT delay antibiotics for aspiration if sepsis present
Lyme arthritis: large joint (knee), may have lower WBC counts

name: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome
signs:
High fever (>104F)
Muscle rigidity (lead-pipe)
Altered mental status
Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis)
Elevated CK
Leukocytosis
Metabolic acidosis
Tremor
treatment:
1. Stop offending agent immediately
2. Aggressive cooling
3. IV fluids
4. Dantrolene for severe rigidity
5. Bromocriptine or amantadine
6. Benzodiazepines for agitation
7. ICU admission
8. Monitor for rhabdomyolysis, renal failure, DIC
meds:
Dantrolene 1-2.5mg/kg IV q6h (max 10mg/kg/day)
Bromocriptine 2.5mg PO/NGT q8h
Lorazepam 1-2mg IV q6h (agitation/rigidity)
IV NS aggressive hydration
Sodium bicarbonate if rhabdomyolysis
ddx: Serotonin syndrome, Malignant hyperthermia, Lethal catatonia, Meningitis/encephalitis, Thyroid storm, Anticholinergic toxicity, Heat stroke
labs:
CK (markedly elevated, often >1000)
BMP (AKI from rhabdomyolysis)
CBC (leukocytosis)
LFTs
Coags (DIC)
Urinalysis (myoglobinuria)
Lactate
ABG/VBG
Iron (low)
Blood cultures (r/o infection)
notes:
Caused by dopamine antagonists: haloperidol, droperidol, metoclopramide, all antipsychotics
Onset: days to weeks after starting or dose increase
NMS vs Serotonin syndrome: NMS = rigidity, slow onset, bradyreflexia; SS = clonus, rapid onset, hyperreflexia
NMS vs Malignant hyperthermia: MH = succinylcholine/inhalational anesthetics, masseter rigidity
Mortality 5-20% with treatment
Reintroduce antipsychotic cautiously (different class) after 2+ weeks

name: Serotonin Syndrome
signs:
Clonus (spontaneous, inducible, ocular)
Agitation/restlessness
Diaphoresis
Hyperthermia
Tachycardia
Hyperreflexia
Tremor
Muscle rigidity (late)
Diarrhea
Dilated pupils
treatment:
1. Stop all serotonergic agents
2. Benzodiazepines for agitation
3. Cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist)
4. Active cooling if hyperthermic
5. IV fluids
6. Avoid paralytics unless intubation needed
7. ICU for severe cases
8. Usually resolves within 24-72h
meds:
Cyproheptadine 12mg PO/NGT then 4mg q2h PRN (max 32mg/day)
Lorazepam 1-2mg IV q15-30min PRN
IV NS hydration
Avoid dantrolene (not effective in SS)
External cooling measures
ddx: NMS, Anticholinergic toxicity, Malignant hyperthermia, Sympathomimetic overdose, Meningitis, Thyroid storm
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (Hunter criteria)
CK (may be elevated)
BMP
CBC
LFTs
Coags
Urinalysis
Lactate
Tox screen
notes:
Hunter criteria: serotonergic agent + ANY of: spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, ocular clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, tremor + hyperreflexia, temp >38 + ocular/inducible clonus
Common drugs: SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, tramadol, triptans, linezolid, methylene blue, dextromethorphan, MDMA
MAOI + SSRI = most dangerous combination
Key differentiator from NMS: clonus and hyperreflexia (NMS has rigidity and hyporeflexia)

name: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / TEN
signs:
Prodrome: fever, malaise, URI symptoms 1-3 days before rash
Target-like lesions evolving to bullae
Flaccid blisters, Nikolsky sign positive
Mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital)
Skin pain/tenderness
Epidermal detachment
SJS: <10% BSA; TEN: >30% BSA
treatment:
1. Stop offending drug immediately
2. Transfer to burn center/ICU
3. Supportive care (wound care, fluid replacement, pain management)
4. Ophthalmology consult (corneal damage)
5. Cyclosporine may reduce progression (evidence emerging)
6. Avoid systemic corticosteroids (controversial)
7. SCORTEN for prognosis
meds:
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
Pain management: morphine, acetaminophen
Cyclosporine 3-5mg/kg/day (controversial but growing evidence)
Silver-based wound dressings
Ophthalmologic drops/lubricants
Avoid sulfonamides, corticosteroids debated
ddx: Erythema multiforme, DRESS syndrome, Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, Pemphigus vulgaris, Autoimmune bullous diseases, Acute GVHD
labs:
Clinical diagnosis
Skin biopsy (full-thickness epidermal necrosis)
CBC, BMP, LFTs
Blood cultures (secondary infection)
Photo documentation
SCORTEN score at day 1 and day 3
notes:
Most common culprits: allopurinol, sulfonamides, carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, NSAIDs (oxicam), antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins)
SCORTEN: age >40, malignancy, BSA >10%, tachycardia, BUN >28, glucose >252, bicarb <20
HLA-B*5801: allopurinol SJS risk (Southeast Asian, African American)
HLA-B*1502: carbamazepine SJS risk (Southeast Asian)
Ocular complications in 50-90%: symblepharon, corneal scarring, dry eye

name: Pre-eclampsia / Eclampsia
signs:
HTN (>=140/90 after 20wk gestation)
Proteinuria
Headache
Visual changes (scotomata)
RUQ/epigastric pain
Edema (facial)
Hyperreflexia/clonus
Seizures (eclampsia)
Pulmonary edema
HELLP: hemolysis, elevated LFTs, low platelets
treatment:
1. Delivery is definitive treatment
2. Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis
3. Antihypertensives for severe HTN (>=160/110)
4. >=37wk: deliver
5. <37wk with severe features: deliver after stabilization
6. <37wk without severe features: expectant management with close monitoring
7. Betamethasone if <34wk (fetal lung maturity)
meds:
Magnesium sulfate 4-6g IV bolus then 1-2g/h infusion
Labetalol 20mg IV then 40mg then 80mg q10min
Hydralazine 5-10mg IV q20min
Nifedipine 10-20mg PO q20-30min
Betamethasone 12mg IM x2 doses 24h apart (if <34wk)
Calcium gluconate 1g IV (magnesium antidote)
ddx: Gestational HTN, Chronic HTN, HELLP syndrome, TTP/HUS, Acute fatty liver of pregnancy, SLE flare, Pheochromocytoma
labs:
BP monitoring
24h urine protein or protein/creatinine ratio
CBC (platelets, hemolysis)
BMP (creatinine, uric acid)
LFTs (AST/ALT)
LDH
Peripheral smear (schistocytes)
Coags
Fetal monitoring (NST, BPP, umbilical artery Doppler)
notes:
